original: San Marino, one of the smallest nations in the world and the world's oldest republic still in existence, is founded by Saint Marinus
Battle of Ain Jalut: Mamluks of Egypt defeat the Mongol army led by Kitbuqa in the Levant, often labeled a turning point in world history, saving the Arabic-Islamic civilization from destruction
Utrecht surrenders to the Habsburg army
Cardinal Beaton replaces the Earl of Arran as regent for Queen Mary of Scots
Battle of Nuremberg: Duke Wallenstein defeats Sweden
Battle of Worcester:Oliver Cromwell's New Model Army destroys the English royalist force, composed mainly of Scots, in the last battle of the English Civil War
Turkish troops break through the defenses of Vienna
King William's War in America ends with the Treaty of Ryswick, the peace treaty ending the Nine Years' War between France and the Grand Alliance, reverting to conditions that existed before the war
First major group of Swiss German colonists reaches North and South Carolina
Britain, France, Hanover, and Prussia sign the Covenant of Hanover
William IV, Prince of Orange, is installed as Stadtholder of Friesland
The Battle of Cooch's Bridge is fought in Newark, Delaware, the only American Revolutionary War battle fought in Delaware
The flag of the United States flies in battle for the first time at Cooch's Bridge, Delaware, during a skirmish in the American Revolutionary War
Earl d'Orvilliers (French-Spanish Armada) sails back to Brest
The Treaty of Paris is signed in Paris, ending the American Revolutionary War between Great Britain and the United States of America
Battle of St. George's Caye: Week-long battle begins between the Spanish Empire and Great Britain off the coast of Belize
The world's first factory to preserve food in tinned iron containers (Donkin, Hall, and Gamble) opens in London, England, to supply food to the Royal Navy
USS Vincennes leaves New York to become the first warship to circumnavigate the globe
Rebellious enslaved people set fire to Paramaribo, Suriname
The Sun, the first successful penny press tabloid daily newspaper in the US, begins publishingin New York City
Anti-Jewish riots break out in Stockholm, Sweden
Indian Wars: 700 soldiers under American General William S. Harney avenge the Grattan Massacre by attacking a Sioux village in Nebraska, killing 100 men, women, and children
Confederate forces enter Kentucky, ending its neutrality
Battle of Berryville, Virginia, Confederate forces retreat in the face of Union strength
US, British, French, and Dutch naval officers sail the Straits of Shimonoseki
Army commander in South Carolina orders Freedmen's Bureau to stop seizing land
The Congress of the State of Mexico elevates Naucalpan to the category of Villa with the title of "Villa de Juárez"
British passenger paddle steamer Princess Alice sinks in a collision on the River Thames with the collier Bywell Castle, killing 645 people
Jesse James' gang robs the Mammoth Cave stagecoach in Kentucky
Cotton pickers organize a union and stage a strike in Texas
John Stephens Durham is named US minister to Haiti
Russian troops now control both sides of the Amur River on the Russo-Manchurian boundary
With a proclamation by General Lord Roberts, Britain annexes the Boer Republic of South Africa
American yacht Reliance, the largest gaff-rigged cutter ever built, defends America's Cup for the NYYC, beating UK challenger Shamrock III off the New Jersey shore for a 3-0 series win

For the first time in Olympic Games history, there is a throw-off in the discus final after Americans Martin Sheridan and Ralph Rose tie with a best throw of 128 ft 10½ in (39.28 m) in St. Louis; Sheridan wins with 127 ft 10¼ in (38.97 m)
After an eight-minute argument over an umpire call, the NY Highlanders win by forfeit over the Philadelphia A's; the Highlanders achieve an MLB record fifth doubleheader sweep on consecutive days
Philadelphia Giants win the Negro Championship Cup in Philadelphia before 10,000 fans, Black baseball's largest crowd ever
James Barrie's "What Every Woman Knows" premieres in London
British Expeditionary Army and General Lanrezac's army attack the Marne
French troops vacate Reims
Lemberg, the capital of Galicia, is taken after a three-day battle in which the Russians rout the Austrians
Prince Wilhelm von Wied leaves Albania
Battle of Verdun: French counterattacks on German flanks push their frontline further from the town
First night bombing of London by German aircraft
German troops overrun Riga, Latvia
Grover Cleveland Alexanderpitches complete games in a doubleheader
Allied forces push German troops back across the Hindenburg Line
Five soldiers are hanged for alleged participation in the Houston riot (or Camp Logan riot); in all, 19 mutineers are executed
General John Smuts becomes the second Prime Minister of the Union of South Africa
The Communist Party of Belgium (KPB) forms
Dorothy Donnelly's "Poppy" premieres in New York City
The airship USS Shenandoah (ZR-1) crashes in a storm near Caldwell, Ohio, killing 14; 29 survive
Dow Jones Industrial Average reaches an all-time high of 381.17, shortly followed by the Crash of 1929
A hurricane kills 2,000 and injures 4,000 in the Dominican Republic
Yevgeniy Abalakov is the first to reach Communism Peak (7,495 m), the highest point in the Soviet Union
Tunisia's movement for independence begins
Andrew Varipapa sets a bowling record of 2,652 points in 10 games
The 1940 Olympic site changes from Tokyo, Japan, to Helsinki, Finland, because of the Second Sino-Japanese War; WWII causes eventual cancellation
German submarine U-30, commanded by Oberleutnant Fritz-Julius Lemp, sinks British passenger ship SS Athenia; 117 people die, including 28 Americans
Mitford sister and Nazi sympathizer Unity Mitford attempts suicide after Britain declares war on Germany; the bullet lodged in her brain eventually kills her in 1948
World War II: Britain declares war on Germany after the invasion of Poland; France follows six hours later, quickly joined by Australia, New Zealand, South Africa, and Canada
39.4 cm of rainfall in Sapulpa, Oklahoma (state record)
Dutch government-in-exile of Pieter Sjoerds Gerbrandy forms in London, England
German security police, the Sicherheitsdienst, ban the Freemasons, Rotary, and Red Cross
US gives Britain 50 destroyers in exchange for a Newfoundland base lease
First use of Zyklon-B gas in Auschwitz on Russian prisoners of war
KYW TV channel 3 in Philadelphia, PA (NBC) begins broadcasting
British 8th Army lands in Messina, Sicily, Italy

Italy and the Allies sign the Armistice of Cassibile, the Italian instrument of surrender, signed by Italian General Giuseppe Castellano and US General Walter Bedell Smith at Cassibile in Sicily and made public five days later
Canadian troops liberate Abbeville, France
French troops liberate Lyon, France
Prince Bernhard is appointed supreme commander of the Netherlands Domestic Armed Forces
Tank division of British Guards frees Brussels, Belgium
World War II: Japanese forces in the Philippines surrender to the Allies
New York Yankees total 18 hits, all singles, to rout the Boston Red Sox 11-2 at Fenway Park
Philadelphia A's rookie pitcher Bill McCahan no-hits the Washington Senators, 3-0
Władysław Gomułka is deposed as General Secretary of the Polish Workers' Party
Fire in Chiang-king, China, kills 7,000 people
TV soap opera "Search for Tomorrow" debuts on CBS
European Convention on Human Rights goes into effect
Espionage and Sabotage Act of 1954 is signed in the US, prompted by the Cold War
The German U-Boat U-505 begins its move from a specially constructed dock to its final site at Chicago's Museum of Science and Industry
The People's Liberation Army begins shelling the ROC-controlled islands of Quemoy and Amoy
KTBS TV channel 3 in Shreveport, LA (ABC) begins broadcasting
Tanks are deployed against racist demonstrators in Clinton, Tennessee
Brooklyn Dodgers play their final game in Jersey City, a 3-2 loss to the Philadelphia Phillies; 11-4 record in New Jersey
KTCA TV channel 2 in St Paul-Minneapolis, MN (PBS) begins broadcasting
Livio Berruti of Italy wins the gold medal in the 200 m in a world record time of 20.5 seconds at the Rome Olympics

US women's 4 x 100 m freestyle relay team of Joan Spillane, Shirley Stobs, Carolyn Wood, and Chris von Saltza swims a world record of 4:08.9 to beat Australia by 2.4 seconds and win the gold medal at the Rome Olympics
Athol Fugard's first major play "The Blood Knot" premieres at Dorkay House, Johannesburg, as the first stage performance with an interracial cast in South Africa
Second incident that year of race riots in Singapore between Chinese and Malay; 13 people are killed and 106 are injured
García Godoy forms a government in the Dominican Republic
Preparing a move to Anaheim, the Los Angeles Angels change their name to the California Angels
Rolling Stones concert at the Adelphi Theatre in Dublin, Ireland, halts after 12 minutes due to a riot
Houston Oilers hold Denver Broncos to no first downs in their NFL season-opening 45-7 win at Rice Stadium
Final episode of "What's My Line?" is hosted by John Charles Daly on CBS TV
WJPM TV channel 33 in Florence, SC (PBS) begins broadcasting
After playing a NL record 1,117 consecutive MLB games, Chicago Cubs outfielder Billy Williams asks to sit out
A baby girl and an Ulster Defence Regiment (UDR) soldier are killed in separate shooting incidents in Northern Ireland
Manlio Brosio resigns as secretary general of NATO
Qatar regains full independence from Britain
Watergate team breaks into Daniel Ellsberg's psychiatrist's office in Los Angeles but fails to find his file
American women's 4 x 100 m medley relay team of Melissa Belote, Cathy Carr, Deena Deardurff, and Sandy Neilson swims a world record 4:20.75 to beat East Germany for gold at the Munich Olympics
Finnish runner Lasse Virén scores a famous victory in the 10,000 m at the Munich Olympics in world record 27:38.35
Great Britain's Mary Peters sets a new world record of 4,801 points to win the Munich Olympics pentathlon gold by just 10 points over Heide Rosendahl of West Germany
Jerry Grant is the first driver to average over 200 mph in a qualifying lap in Champ Car history, achieving a speed of 201.414 mph at Ontario Motor Speedway
Swedish swimmer Gunnar Larsson wraps up the Olympic medley double as he sets a world record of 2:07.17 in the 200 m individual medley in Munich, having already won the 400m I/M
General Walters ends his term as acting director of the CIA
Jerry Lewis's 8th Muscular Dystrophy Telethon
SF Giants pitcher John Montefusco, in his MLB debut, hits a home run in his first at-bat and pitches nine innings in relief to earn a 9-5 victory over the Dodgers
The US and the German Democratic Republic establish diplomatic relations
Chartered Boeing 707 crashes in Atlas Mountains of Morocco, killing 188
England cricket batsman Bob Woolmer scores 149 on the final day to save the 4th Test against Australia at The Oval in the longest first-class match played in England, lasting 32 hours and 17 minutes
Viking 2 soft-lands on Mars (Utopia) and returns photos
Japanese baseball superstar Sadaharu Oh hits his 756th career home run to surpass Hank Aaron as the all-time career leader in professional baseball
Crew of Soyuz 31 returns to Earth aboard Soyuz 29
Pope John Paul I is officially installed as the 263rd Supreme Pontiff
Hurricane David, a strong Atlantic storm, kills over 1,000 people
Iran army conquers Mahabad
Jerry Lewis's 14th Muscular Dystrophy telethon raises $30,000,000
Longest MLB game in Fenway Park history is suspended after 19 innings with Seattle Mariners 7, Boston Red Sox 7; Mariners win 8-7 in the 20th inning the following morning
Anker Jørgensen government in Denmark resigns
28-year-old Chicagoan wins $40 million in Illinois State Lottery
Jerry Lewis's 19th Muscular Dystrophy telethon raises $32,074,566

MLB St. Louis Cardinals closer Bruce Sutter breaks his own NL record for saves in a season with his 45th in a 7-3 win over the NY Mets; the record is broken in 1991 by Lee Smith of the St. Louis Cardinals
South Africa adopts a constitution
20th NASA Space Shuttle Mission (51-I): Discovery 6 returns to Earth
Houston Astros and Chicago Cubs use an MLB record of 53 players, including 17 pitchers, in a two-day, 18-inning marathon; Astros win 8-7
A coup in Burundi suspends the constitution
An estimated 50,000 Kurdish civilians and soldiers are killed by Iraq by this date, many using chemical weapons, in the aftermath of the Iran-Iraq War
Ilyushin-62 crashes into a residential area of Havana, killing 170 people
Helen Hudson sings the national anthem in the 26th park of the year, San Diego
Jerry Lewis's 25th Muscular Dystrophy telethon raises $44,172,186
Jerry Lewis's 27th Muscular Dystrophy telethon raises $45,759,368

Miami routs Georgia Southern 56-0, breaking the NCAA football record with its 58th consecutive home victory, surpassing the previous record of 57 straight home wins held by Alabama, who achieved this streak between 1963 and 1982
Terry Dean of Florida throws an NCAA football record 7 touchdown passes in the first half as the Gators roll to a 70-21 drubbing of New Mexico State
Carolina Panthers lose their debut NFL game in overtime 23-20 to the Atlanta Falcons in front of 58,808 fans at Georgia Dome
eBay (Electronic Bay) is founded by Pierre Omidyar
In their NFL debut game, the Jacksonville Jaguars fall to the Houston Oilers 10-3; 72,363 fans attend Jacksonville Municipal Stadium
Soyuz TM-22 launches into orbit
David Slowinski and Paul Gage discover 2^1257787 - 1, the 34th known Mersenne prime
Vietnam Airlines Flight 815, operated by a Tupolev TU-134, crashes on approach to Phnom Penh Airport, killing 65 with one survivor
In just his 11th start, St. Louis Cardinals' rookie pitcher Bud Smith no-hits the San Diego Padres, 4-0
Beslan school massacre, an Islamist terrorist attack in North Ossetia–Alania, Russia, ends in the deaths of approximately 344 people, mostly teachers and children
After beating South Africa the previous weekend, New Zealand clinches its 6th Tri Nations Rugby Series with a 34-24 win over Australia in Auckland; All Blacks winger Doug Howlett crosses for 3 tries
Los Angeles Sparks center Lisa Leslie wins the WNBA Most Valuable Player award, joining Sheryl Swoopes as the league's only three-time winners
Panama Canal's "Third Set of Locks" expansion project begins with a massive initial explosion under Paraíso Hill, watched by thousands of people
The new Oklahoma City NBA franchise announces the team will be named the "Thunder" in reference to powerful storms in the area
Pedro Martínezbecomes the 10th pitcher in history to win at least 100 games in each league
New Zealand announces withdrawal of its forces from Afghanistan
Three people are killed and 19 wounded by a car bombing in Peshawar, Pakistan
Typhoon Bolaven kills 48 people in North Korea
Fifteen militants are killed by an Egyptian Army helicopter in Sinai Peninsula
Battle of Naulochus: Admiral ofOctavian,Marcus Vipsanius Agrippadefeats son of Pompey, Sextus Pompeius, ending Pompeian resistance to the Second Triumvirate
Roman nobleGregory Ibegins his reign as Catholic Pope
Richard the Lionheartis crowned in Westminster, and 30 Jews are massacred after the coronation; Richard orders the perpetrators to be executed
Nicolás de Ovando y Cáceres is appointed Governor of the Indies by Spanish QueenIsabella I
Oliver Cromwell's English New Model Army defeats a Scottish force in a surprise attack at the Battle of Dunbar
Richard Cromwell("Tumbledown Dick") succeeds his father as Lord Protector of the Commonwealth
The new French Constitution, declaring France a constitutional monarchy, is passed by the National Assembly during theFrench Revolution
Frederick Douglassescapes from slavery disguised as a sailor
Anton Brucknercompletes his 6th Symphony
GeneralJan Smutsenters Kiba Drift during the first Boer raid into Cape Colony
Canadian world heavyweight boxing championTommy Burnsknocks out Australian Bill Lang in six rounds in Melbourne in a warm-up fight for his famous title bout with Jack Johnson
Arnold Schoenberg's "Fünf Orchesterstücke" (Five Pieces for Orchestra) premieres in London at a Promenade Concert
Cardinal Giacomo della Chiesa becomes PopeBenedict XV
US PresidentWoodrow Wilsonsigns the Adamson Act, providing an 8-hour workday for interstate railroad workers and preventing a national railroad strike
Laurence Stallings andMaxwell Anderson's "What Price Glory?" premieres in New York City
Future Baseball Hall of Fame outfielderTy Cobbrecords his 4,189th and final career hit as a pinch hitter for the Philadelphia A's in a 6-1 loss against the Washington Senators
A's first basemanJimmie Foxxsmashes his 50th and 51st home runs to become only the third player to reach 50 in an MLB season, joining Babe Ruth andHack Wilson
British racerMalcolm Campbellpowers Bluebird to 301.129 mph at the Bonneville Salt Flats, Utah, becoming the first automobile to exceed 300 mph
Francisco Franco's troops conquer Irún and Talavera de la Reina, Spain
Adolf Hitlerorders an invasion of Great Britain for September 21 (Operation Seelöwe/Sealion)
Holocaust diaristAnne Frank(15) is among the prisoners on the 68th and final transport of Dutch Jews sent from Westerbrooksent to Auschwitz concentration camp
Giuseppe "Nino" Farina wins the inaugural Formula 1 World Drivers' Championship by winning the Italian Grand Prix at Monza in an Alfa Romeo, finishing three points ahead ofJuan Manuel Fangio
French ministerFrançois Mitterrandresigns due to colonial policy
AmericanJohnny Longdenbecomes thoroughbred racing's winningest rider, breaking the record of 4,870 wins by British jockey Sir Gordon Richards; rides Arrogate to victory in the Del Mar Handicap
Milwaukee Braves pitcherWarren Spahnsets an NL record for a left-hander with his 41st shutout, beating the Cubs 8-0
US attorney generalRobert F. Kennedyresigns
Wilderness Act is signed into law by PresidentLyndon B. Johnson
PopePaul VIpublishes encyclical Mysterium Fidei
24th World Science Fiction Convention honors "Star Trek" creatorGene Roddenberry
Donovanhits #1 with "Sunshine Superman"
Future Hockey Hall of FamerBobby Orrsigns his first NHL contract with the Boston Bruins, a two-year deal paying a then-record $70,000 plus a signing bonus
Nguyễn Văn Thiệuis elected President of South Vietnam under a new constitution
Bill Haley& His Comets reject $30,000 for a 15-date tour of Australia
Indonesian presidentSuhartovisits the Netherlands
American swimmer Keena Rothhammer swims a world record 8:53.68 to beat Australian wonder-kidShane Gouldin the 800 m at the Munich Olympics

American swimmerMark Spitzwins the coveted 100 m gold medal in a world record time of 51.22 at the Munich Olympics and completes the freestyle sprint double, having already won the 200 m in a world record time of 1:52.78
Atlanta Braves sluggerHank Aaronearns his 6,135th total base to breakStan Musial’s MLB record in an 8-0 home loss to the Philadelphia Phillies
Future Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame guardOscar Robertsonretires, leaving the NBA with 26,710 points, 9,887 assists, and 7,804 rebounds in 1,040 games
Dodgers first basemanSteve Garveybegins his National League record 1,207 consecutive game streak in a 13-2 loss against the Cincinnati Reds
NY Mets catcherGary Cartersmashes three consecutive home runs in an 8-3 win against San Diego Padres at Jack Murphy Stadium
Alan Ayckbourn's "Woman in Mind" premieres in London
White Sox closer Bobby Thigpen topsDave Righetti's MLB record with his 47th save in Chicago's 4-2 win against the Royals; Thigpen finishes the season with 57 saves
Mario Lemieux's ownership group officially takes over the NHL Pittsburgh Penguins; Lemieux is the first player in the modern era of sports to buy the team he once played for
Pedro Martínezreturns from the disabled list, wins his 207th career game, and collects his 3,000th career strikeout
Britain and the British Empire (including the American colonies) adopt the Gregorian Calendar, losing 11 days; people riot, thinking the government has stolen 11 days of their lives
PresidentWoodrow Wilsonsets out on a tour of the USA to rouse public opinion behind the Treaty of Versailles and the League of Nations
Sweden begins driving on the right-hand side of the road (Dagen H)
Go-Sanjō [Takahito], 71st Emperor of Japan (1068-73), born in Heian Kyō, Japan (d. 1073)
Diane de Poitiers, French mistress of Henry II of France, born in Saint-Vallier, Drôme, France (d. 1566)
Adriano Banchieri, Italian organist, composer, poet, and Benedictine monk, born in Bologna, Papal States (d. 1634)
Michel Sarrazin, French surgeon and naturalist (kings physician in New France, discovered sarsaparilla), baptized at Gilly-lès-Cîteaux, France (d. 1734)[1]
Paul Dudley, Attorney-General of Massachusetts, born in Roxbury, Massachusetts (d. 1751)
Charles Radclyffe, 5th Earl of Derwentwater, British politician, born in Little Parndon, Essex (d. 1746)
Pietro Locatelli, Italian violinist and composer, born in Bergamo, Republic of Venice (d. 1764)
Johann Theodor of Bavaria, Prince-Bishop of Regensburg, Freising and Liège, born in Munich, Germany (d. 1763)
Abraham Trembley, Swiss naturalist (first to study freshwater polyps or hydra), born in Geneva, Switzerland (d. 1784)
Ferdinand Zellbell the younger, Swedish composer and co-founder of the Royal Swedish Academy of Music, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1780)
Guy Carleton, 1st Baron Dorchester, British soldier and administrator (Governor of Quebec 1768-1778), born in Strabane, Ireland (d. 1808)
John Macarthur, Australian pastoralist, helped found wool industry in Australia, baptized at Stoke Damerel, England (d. 1834)[1]
Jean Nicolas Auguste Kreutzer, French violinist and composer, born in Versailles, France (d. 1832)
Edmund Passy, Swedish composer, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1870)
Francisco Acuña de Figueroa, Uruguayan poet (Toraidas), born in Montevideo, Uruguay (d. 1862)
Alexander Gurilyov, Russian composer, born in Moscow, Russia (d. 1858)
Prudence Crandall, American educator and founder of School for "young ladies of colour", born in Hopkinton, Rhode Island (d. 1890)
Paul Kane, Irish-Canadian painter, born in Mallow, County Cork, Ireland (d. 1871)
John Humphrey Noyes, American preacher and founded the Oneida Community (Perfectionists), born in Brattleboro, Vermont (d. 1886)
George Hearst, American businessman and politician (U.S. Senator from California), born in Sullivan, Missouri (d. 1891)
Armistead Lindsay Long, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Campbell County, Virginia (d. 1891)
William Wallace Burns, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Coshocton, Ohio (d. 1892)
William Gaston Lewis, American engineer and general in the Confederate Army, born in Rocky Mount, North Carolina (d. 1901)
Jacob Christian Fabricius, Danish composer, born in Aarhus, Denmark (d. 1919)
Tom Emmett, England cricket fast bowler (7 Tests, famous roundarm action), born in Halifax, Yorkshire (d. 1904)
Sarah Orne Jewett, American author (Country of the Pointed Firs), born in South Berwick, Maine (d. 1909)
Olga Constantinovna of Russia, Queen consort of the Hellenes as the wife of King George I, born in Pavlovsk, Russian Empire (d. 1926)
Jean Jaurès, French socialist (L'Humanité, Les Preuves), born in Castres, Second French Empire (d. 1914)
Edward A. Filene, American entrepreneur and philanthropist, born in Salem, Massachusetts (d. 1937)
Hale A. VanderCook, American composer, born in Ann Arbor, Michigan (d. 1949)
Wilhelm Bousset, German theologist and historian, born in the Free Imperial City of Lübeck (d. 1920)
Dorothea Lambert Chambers, British tennis player (Wimbledon 1903-04, 06, 10-11, 13-14), born in Ealing, England (d. 1960)
Johnny Douglas, English cricket all-rounder, captain (23 Tests, 1 x 100, 45 wickets; Essex CCC) and boxer (Olympic gold middleweight 1908), born in London, England (d. 1930)
Frank Christian, American jazz trumpeter, born in Bywater, New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1973)
Cecil Weston, South African actress (Dude Ranch, Huckleberry Finn), born in Capetown (d. 1976)
Bessie [Annie Elizabeth] Delany, American dentist and civil rights pioneer, born in Raleigh, North Carolina (d. 1995)
Florrie Rodrigo, Dutch dancer, choreographer, and teacher (Schepelingen), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1996)
Cecil Parker, British actor (The Lady Vanishes, Indiscreet, Tale of 2 Cities), born in Hastings, East Sussex, England (d. 1971)
Francisco Mignone, Brazilian composer, born in São Paulo, Brazil (d. 1986)
Sally Benson, American writer (Meet Me In St. Louis), born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1972)
Frank Macfarlane Burnet, Australian virologist (Nobel Prize for Physiology or Medicine for tissue grafting), born in Traralgon, Victoria (d. 1985)
Percy Chapman, English cricket batsman (26 Tests, captain 1926-31, 1 x 100; Kent CCC), born in Reading, England (d. 1961)
Urho Kekkonen, 8th President of Finland (1956-81), born in Pielavesi, Finland (d. 1986)
Eduard van Beinum, Dutch musician and conductor, born in Arnhem, Netherlands (d. 1959)
Mantan Moreland, American actor and comedian (Charlie Chan, Spirit of Youth), born in Monroe, Louisiana (d. 1973)
Willem Kooiman, Dutch theologian and church historian renowned for his studies on Martin Luther, born in Barneveld, Netherlands (d. 1968)[1]
John Mills, New Zealand cricket batsman (7 Tests, 241 runs, 1 x 100), born in Dunedin, New Zealand (d. 1972)
Robert Ruthenfranz, German composer, born in Witten, Germany (d. 1970)
Andrew Brewin, Canadian lawyer and MP for Greenwood, born in Brighton, England (d. 1983)
Loren Eiseley, American anthropologist and natural science writer (The Unexpected Universe), and poet (Another Kind of Autumn), born in Lincoln, Nebraska (d. 1977)
Lev Pontryagin, Russian mathematician, born in Moscow, Russian Empire (d. 1988)
Dorothy Maynor, American soprano and founder (Harlem School of Arts), born in Norfolk, Virginia (d. 1996)
Kitty Carlisle, American actress and television personality, born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 2007)
Alan Ladd, American actor (Shane, Carpetbaggers), born in Hot Springs, Arkansas (d. 1964)
Dixy Lee Ray, American politician (17th Governor of Washington) and chairman of the United States Atomic Energy Commission (AEC), born in Tacoma, Washington (d. 1994)
Abel Ehrlich, Israeli composer, born in Cranz, East Prussia (d. 2003)
Knut Nystedt, Norwegian organist, choral composer, and conductor, born in Kristiania, Norway (d. 2014)
Memphis Slim [John Len Chatman], American blues musician ("Every Day I Have the Blues"), born in Memphis, Tennessee (d. 1988)
Clyde Hurley, American session and big band jazz trumpeter (Glenn Miller - "Tuxedo Junction"; "In The Mood"), born in Fort Worth, Texas (d. 1963)

Eddie Stanky, American baseball 2nd baseman (MLB All Star 1947, 48, 50; Brooklyn Dodgers, Boston Braves, NY Giants) and manager (St. Louis Cardinals, Chicago White Sox, Texas Rangers), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1999)
Helen Wagner, American actress (Mister Peepers, As The World Turns), born in Lubbock, Texas (d. 2010)
Harry Landers [Sorokin], American actor (Star Trek, Ben Casey, Drive a Crooked Road), born in New York City (d. 2017)
John Aston Sr., English soccer striker (17 caps; Manchester United 253 games), born in Prestwich, England (d. 2003)
Marguerite Higgins, American reporter, 1st woman to win a Pulitzer Prize for Foreign Correspondence, born in Hong Kong (d. 1966)[1]
Thurston Dart, English harpsichordist and conductor, born in Surbiton, London (d. 1971)
Morrie Martin, American MLB baseball pitcher, 1949-59 (Brooklyn Dodgers, Philadelphia Athletics, and 5 other teams), and double Purple Heart Army veteran, born in Dixon, Missouri (d. 2010)
Rosendo Ejercito Santos, Filipino composer, born in Caridad, Cavite City, Philippines (d. 1994)

Salli Terri [Stella Tirri], Canadian-American Grammy Award-winning pop-classical-folk-exotica singer, arranger, composer (Duets with the Spanish Guitar), musicologist, and educator, born in London, Ontario (d. 1996)
Steffan Danielsen, Faroese painter, mostly of local landscapes, born in Nólsoy, Faroe Islands (d. 1976)
Fred Hawkins, American golfer (US Masters 1958 runner-up), born in Antioch, Illinois (d. 2014)
Herbert Binkert, German soccer striker (12 caps Saarland; FC Saarbrücken), born in Karlsruhe, Germany (d. 2020)
Mort Walker, American cartoonist (Beetle Bailey, Hi & Lois), born in El Dorado, Kansas (d. 2018)
Terry Wilson, American actor (Wagon Train, Escape to Witch Mountain), born in Huntington Park, California (d. 1999)
Bengt Lindström, Swedish artist, born in Berg Municipality, Sweden (d. 2008)
Shoista Mullodzhanova, Shashmakom singer, born in Dushanbe, Tajik ASSR, Uzbek, Soviet Union (d. 2010)
Alison Lurie, American novelist (Pulitzer Prize - Foreign Affairs), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2020)
Anne Jackson, American stage actress (Twice Around the Park), born in Millvale, Pennsylvania (d. 2016)
Bill Flemming, American journalist and sportscaster (ABC's Wide World of Sports), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2007)
Ernie Henry, American jazz saxophonist (Fats Navarro; Dizzy Gillespie; Illinois Jacquet), born in Brooklyn, New York City (d. 1957)
Irene Papas [neé Lelekou], Greek actress (Zorba the Greek; Attila The Hun; The Guns of Navarone), born in Chiliomodi, Corinth, Greece (d. 2022)
John Robert Jones [John Dalmas], American sci-fi author (White Regiment, Lizard War), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2017)
Joseph P. Kolter, American politician (US Representatives from Pennsylvania, 1983-93), born in McDonald, Ohio (d. 2019)
Hugh Sidey, American journalist (Life; Time), and author (Hugh Sidey's Portraits of the Presidents), born in Greenfield, Iowa (d. 2005)
Gaston Thorn, 20th Prime Minister of Luxembourg (1974–79) and 7th President of the European Commission (1981–85), born in Luxembourg (d. 2007)
Carlo Clerici, Swiss road cyclist (Giro d'Italia 1954), born in Vaiano, Italy (d. 2007)
Catherine Baker Knoll, American politician (30th Lieutenant Governor of Pennsylvania, 2003-08 - 1st female to hold the office), born in McKees Rocks, Pennsylvania (d. 2008)
Cherry Barbara Grimm [Lockett], New Zealand sci-fi author (Nearest Fire), born in Auckland, New Zealand (d. 2002)
Albert DeSalvo, American criminal (The Boston Strangler), born in Chelsea, Massachusetts (d. 1973)
Dick Motta, American basketball coach (NBA Championship, Washington Bullets 1977-78), born in Midvale, Utah
Rudolf Kelterborn, Swiss composer, conductor (Swiss German RSO, 1974-80), and educator (Basel Music Academy, 1983-94), born in Basel, Switzerland (d. 2021)
(Granville) "Mickey" Roker, American jazz drummer (Art Farmer; Milt Jackson; Dizzy Gillespie), born in Miami, Florida (d. 2017)
